coredata.dc:title: g-C3N4/AgBr nanocomposite decorated with carbon dots as a highly efficient visible-light-driven photocatalyst
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Miao, Xuli
Ji, Zhenyuan
Wu, Jiajia
Shen, Xiaoping
Wang, Jiheng
Kong, Lirong
Liu, Miaomiao
Song, Chunsen
coredata.dc:description: Visible-light-driven photocatalysis as a green technology has attracted intense interest due to its potential applications in environmental remediation. However, the poor visible light utilization and low electron-hole separation efficiency of photocatalysts largely limited their practical application. In this work, a new ternary visible-light driven photocatalyst of g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr has been prepared by the introduction of carbon dots (CDs) onto the surface of g-C3N4, followed by in-situ growth of AgBr nanoparticles on CDs-modified g-C3N4 nanosheets. The g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr nanocomposite exhibits excellent photocatalytic efficiency for organic pollutant degradation, which is about 4.0, 5.3 and 2.3 times higher than that of AgBr, g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/AgBr, respectively. The result indicates the introduction of CDs into g-C3N4/AgBr can largely improve the photocatalytic activity since CDs act as the light absorber and the electron mediator between g-C3N4 and AgBr, which effectively promote the separation of photogenerated charge carriers and the utilization of visible light. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr has no obvious decrease after four photodegradation cycles, demonstrating a high photocatalytic stability. This study highlights the potential application of highly efficient CDs decorated photocatalysts in waste water purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4
AgBr
Carbon dots
Nanocomposite
Photocatalysis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of the as-prepared samples. (b) A close up view of the (002) diffraction peak of g-C3N4 in pristine g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CDs.
TEM images of the as-prepared (a) g-C3N4, (b) CDs, (c) g-C3N4/CDs, (d) g-C3N4/AgBr and (e, f) g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr nanocomposite.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) O 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) C 1s, (e) Ag 3d and (f) Br 3d for g-C3N4/AgBr/CDs-3 nanocomposite.
(a) FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared AgBr, g-C3N4, CDs, g-C3N4/AgBr and g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr; (b) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of AgBr, g-C3N4, g-C3N4/CDs, g-C3N4/AgBr and g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr.
PL spectra of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/CDs, g-C3N4/AgBr and g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr.

(a) The photocatalytic degradation of RhB based on the as-prepared g-C3N4, AgBr, g-C3N4/CDs, g-C3N4/AgBr, g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-1, g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-2, g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-3, g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-4 and g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-5 photocatalysts; (b) Pseudo-first-order kinetic curves of the photocatalytic degradation of RhB with different photocatalysts; (c) The rate constants (k) of the photodegradation with different photocatalysts; (d) Cycle experiments of g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-3 for photocatalytic degradation of RhB under visible-light irradiation.
Transient photocurrent response curves of g-C3N4, AgBr, g-C3N4/CDs, g-C3N4/AgBr and g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-3.
The trapping experiments of the active species for the photocatalytic reaction based on g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-3 photocatalyst.

ESR spectra of (a) DMPO-
O
2 -
adducts in methanol solution and (b) DMPO-
OH
adducts in aqueous solution recorded with g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr composite during light irradiation.
The proposed photocatalytic mechanism for photodegradation of RhB based on g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr photocatalysts.
Schematic illustration of preparation process of the g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: g-C3N4/AgBr nanocomposite decorated with carbon dots as a highly efficient visible-light-driven photocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Visible-light-driven photocatalysis as a green technology has attracted intense interest due to its potential applications in environmental remediation. However, the poor visible light utilization and low electron-hole separation efficiency of photocatalysts largely limited their practical application. In this work, a new ternary visible-light driven photocatalyst of g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr has been prepared by the introduction of carbon dots (CDs) onto the surface of g-C3N4, followed by in-situ growth of AgBr nanoparticles on CDs-modified g-C3N4 nanosheets. The g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr nanocomposite exhibits excellent photocatalytic efficiency for organic pollutant degradation, which is about 4.0, 5.3 and 2.3 times higher than that of AgBr, g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/AgBr, respectively. The result indicates the introduction of CDs into g-C3N4/AgBr can largely improve the photocatalytic activity since CDs act as the light absorber and the electron mediator between g-C3N4 and AgBr, which effectively promote the separation of photogenerated charge carriers and the utilization of visible light. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr has no obvious decrease after four photodegradation cycles, demonstrating a high photocatalytic stability. This study highlights the potential application of highly efficient CDs decorated photocatalysts in waste water purification.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of modern industrialization, the environmental pollution problem has been becoming an increasing challenge [1]. Several methods such as adsorption [2], electrochemistry [3] and photocatalysis [4] have been taken to remove and degrade organic pollutants in wastewater. Among them, photocatalytic technology has attracted intense interest as it provides a green and efficient route for environmental remediation. Up to now, a great number of semiconductor photocatalysts have been explored to enhance the visible-light absorption and improve the photogenerated charge carriers separation, which are two main factors influencing the photocatalytic efficiency [5–10]. Although some significant progress has been made to date, it is still a challenge to develop highly efficient visible-light driven photocatalysts for practical application.

Recently, the polymeric photocatalyst of g-C3N4 made of earth abundant elements has attracted great attention because of its suitable band gap (2.7eV), low cost and favorable thermal and chemical stability [11]. Nevertheless, due to the fast charge recombination, low electrical conductivity and the poor visible-light absorption (<470nm), the practical photocatalytic application of g-C3N4 is still hindered [12,13]. Several strategies, such as element doping [14,15], nano-structure design [16] and the formation of g-C3N4-based heterojunction [17], have been taken to increase the visible-light absorption ability and promote the electron–hole pair separation of g-C3N4 photocatalysts. For example, several type-II g-C3N4-based heterojunctions such as g-C3N4/TiO2 [18], g-C3N4/Ag2CO3 [17], g-C3N4/BiOCl [19], g-C3N4/Fe2O3 [20] have been prepared to realize effective separation of photogenerated electrons and holes.

In addition, due to the great electron conductivity of noble metals, noble metal nanoparticles such as Ag, Au and Pt have been used as the electron mediators between two semiconductors to further improve the separation of the electron–hole pair and to enhance the photocatalytic performance [21,22,16]. Compared to noble metal nanoparticles, carbon dots (CDs), which are primarily composed of graphitic carbon (sp2 carbon) with a size below 10nm, may also act as the electron mediator between two semiconductors due to its high electron conductivity and much lower cost [23]. In addition, CDs show upconversion photoluminescence properties [24]. When CDs coupled with other semiconductors such as g-C3N4 [25], TiO2 [26], ZnO [27] as light absorbers, the composites could absorb a longer wavelength visible light and then emit a shorter wavelength ultraviolet light. As a result, the photocatalysts could excite more charge carriers, and thus improve the photocatalytic efficiency.

In this paper, with CDs as electron mediator between type-II g-C3N4 and AgBr heterojunction, we prepared a new ternary g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr photocatalyst. The CDs decorated photocatalyst shows excellent photocatalytic performance for the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic mechanism of g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr is investigated and the role of CDs played in this photocatalytic system is discussed.

All of the chemical reagents employed in this study were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd, which are of analytical-grade, and used as received without further purification. The water used in the experiment is deionized (DI) water. Carbon dots were prepared by a previously reported method [24].

Preparation of g-C3N4 nanosheets: The bulk g-C3N4 was prepared by a simple calcination approach according to the literature [28]. Typically, 10g of urea powder was put into an alumina crucible with a cover, and successively heated at 250°C for 1h, 350°C for 1h and 550°C for 2h with a heating rate of 2°C/min in air atmosphere to obtain bulk g-C3N4. Subsequently, the obtained bulk g-C3N4 was thermally exfoliated into g-C3N4 nanosheets by calcination at 600°C for 2h at air atmosphere. Finally, the faint yellow product was collected and then grounded into powder with an agate mortar for further use.

Preparation of g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr: In a typical synthesis, 80mg of the as-prepared g-C3N4 was dispersed in 40mL of DI water with ultrasonication. Then, a certain amount of CDs aqueous dispersion (1mg/mL) was added in and stirred for 24h to get a homogeneous CDs/g-C3N4 dispersion. After that, 28.8mL of AgNO3 (1mg/mL) aqueous solution was dropwise added to the suspension, and magnetically stirred for 3h to enable sufficient adsorption of Ag+ ions on the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets. Finally, 40mL of NaBr solution (1mg/mL) was dripped into the reaction system under magnetic stirring, and the mixture was continuously stirred for another 3h. The final product was collected by centrifugation, washed three times with DI water and ethanol, respectively, and then dried in vacuum at 45°C for 12h. The obtained samples were denoted as g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-1, g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-2, g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-3, g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-4 and g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-5 for the CDs feeding amount of 0.05, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5 and 1mL, respectively. For comparison, AgBr/g-C3N4 was prepared in the same way in the absence of CDs. g-C3N4/CDs was obtained as above using 0.3mL of CDs dispersion, and pure AgBr was prepared by directly mixing AgNO3 and NaBr aqueous solutions in the absence of CDs and g-C3N4. The preparation process of the g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr nanocomposites is depicted in Scheme 1 .

The crystalline structures of synthetic samples were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 ADVANCE) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ=1.5406Å) at a scanning rate of 7° min−1. The morphology and sizes of the samples were observed by TEM (JEM-2100). UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy was performed using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2450, Japan) in the range of 200–800nm by using BaSO4 as a reflectance standard material. The infrared absorption spectra of as-prepared samples were recorded by a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet Nexus 470). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer. Elemental analysis for C and N was performed at a Perkin-Elmer 240C microanalysis instrument. The specific surface area (BET) and pore volume was collected at 77K using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 apparatus and porosity analyzer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the photocatalysts were recorded by using a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrometer under an excitation wavelength of 374nm. The ESR signals of radicals spin-trapped by spin-trap reagent 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were examined on a Bruker EPR A300-10/12 spectrometer.

The photodegradation performance of the as-prepared samples was evaluated by the degradation of 10mg/L RhB aqueous solution. A 250W Xe lamp (Science and Education Equipment Co., Ltd, Yangzhou, China) with a 420nm cutoff filter was used as the visible light source. In a typical photocatalytic experiment, 30mg of the as-prepared photocatalyst was dispersed in 60mL of RhB aqueous solution, and all the experiments were performed at room temperature under constant stirring with the temperature maintained at 25°C by flowing cooling water during the photocatalytic process. Prior to irradiation, the solution was magnetically stirred in the dark for 1h to achieve an adsorption–desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and RhB. During every 10min irradiation interval, 3mL of suspension was withdraw regularly and centrifuged, and then the supernatant was analyzed to determine its residual concentration by an UV–vis spectrophotometer. The characteristic absorption wavelength of RhB was chosen to be 554nm.

An CHI 760D electrochemical workstation (ChenHua Instruments, Shanghai, China) with a standard three-electrode system was used to perform the electrochemical measurements and platinum wire and saturated Ag/AgCl electrode were used as the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively. The 0.5M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte in the system. The working electrodes are the FTO glass deposited with as-prepared samples with a light irradiation area of 1.0cm−2. Typically, the working electrode was prepared as follows: Firstly, 8mg of as-prepared samples was dispersed in a mixed solution of 950μL of ethanol and 50μL of Nafion by sonication to form a homogeneous suspension. Then 0.2mL of above suspension was coated onto FTO glass sheet and dried at 45°C overnight to ensure the samples were tightly attached on the FTO. A 300W Xe lamp was utilized as the light source and the distance between illumination source and the photoelectrode was fixed to be 25cm.

Fig. 1 a shows the XRD patterns of the as-prepared g-C3N4, AgBr, g-C3N4/CDs, g-C3N4/AgBr and g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr. For g-C3N4, a strong peak appears at 27.7°, which can be indexed to the (002) facet of g-C3N4 and is a characteristic interlayer-stacking peak of the CN melon networks [29]. After carbon dots are modified on the surface of g-C3N4, the g-C3N4/CDs sample shows a similar XRD pattern with g-C3N4, suggesting that modification of carbon dots does not destroy the structure of g-C3N4. For pure AgBr, the diffraction peaks appear at 26.8°, 31.0°, 44.4°, 55.1° and 64.6°, which correspond to the (111), (200), (220), (222) and (400) planes of cubic phase AgBr (JCPDS No. 06-0438), respectively [30]. The g-C3N4/AgBr and g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr show similar XRD patterns, in which besides the main diffraction peaks originate from AgBr, a weak peak corresponding to (002) plane of g-C3N4 can be observed. The weak g-C3N4 peak could be attributed to the relatively weak crystallization of g-C3N4 in the hybrid system [31]. Meanwhile, the close up view of the (002) diffraction peak of g-C3N4 is shown in Fig. 1b, it is clear that the peak position shifted from 27.73° to 27.55° when carbon dots were introduced into g-C3N4, suggesting the existence of strong interfacial interaction between the CDs and g-C3N4, which led to slight crystal lattice distortion of g-C3N4 [32]. In addition, the modification of g-C3N4 by CDs was also evidenced by the result of elemental analyses (Table S1). Compared with the pristine g-C3N4, the lower N/C molar ratio in the g-C3N4/CDs sample suggests the presence of CDs.

The morphology and microstructure of as-prepared g-C3N4, g-C3N4/CDs, g-C3N4/AgBr and g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr were revealed by TEM. Fig. 2 a shows the TEM image of g-C3N4. It can be seen that the g-C3N4 shows thin wrinkled nanosheet structure, which could result in larger specific surface area and more reactive sites than bulk g-C3N4. Fig. 2b shows that the size of the as-prepared carbon dots is approximately 5nm. As can be seen from Fig. 2c, in the g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite, the carbon dots are well anchored on the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets. It can be seen from Fig. 2d that in the g-C3N4/AgBr nanocomposite, AgBr nanoparticles are deposited on the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets. For the g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr nanocomposite, it can be seen from Fig. 2e and f that AgBr nanoparticles are exclusively deposited on the surface of CDs-modified g-C3N4 nanosheets.

XPS analysis was conducted to investigate the elemental composition and chemical status of the g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr sample, and the result is shown in Fig. 3 . From the XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 3a, the elements of C, N, O, Ag and Br were detected in g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr, well consistent with the composition of the composite. In the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3b), two deconvoluted peaks at 531.3eV and 532.6eV can be ascribed to OH bonds and CO bnds, respectively [33]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.5eV, 399.1eV and 400.6eV, respectively, which can be attributed to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N in g-C3N4 [33]. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) shows four deconvoluted peaks at 284.8eV, 285.8eV and 288.4eV, which can be ascribed to CC bonds, CO bonds and defect-containing sp2-bonded carbon (NCN), respectively. In Fig. 3e, the Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 peaks at 367.7eV and 373.7eV can be ascribed to Ag+ in AgBr [30]. In addition, the Br 3d spectrum (Fig. 3f) shows Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2 peaks at 68.5 and 69.4eV, respectively, which is in good agreement with Br− in AgBr [21].

Fig. 4 a shows the FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared samples. In the FT-IR spectrum of g-C3N4, the sharp peak at 809cm−1 is ascribed to the typical characteristic breathing mode of the tri-s-triazine units. The bands at 1320 and 1241cm−1 represent the stretching vibrations of CNH-C bridges, and several strong peaks at 1412, 1563, and 1634cm−1 are assigned to CN and CN heterocycles skeletal vibration of the aromatic ring [32]. In the FT-IR spectrum of CDs, the stretching vibrations of CH at 2923cm−1 and 2850cm−1, bending vibrations of CH at 1570cm−1, and the vibrational absorption band of CO at 1635cm−1 are observed, which are consistent with the previous reports [34]. Because of the weak absorptions of AgBr can be covered by those strong ones from g-C3N4, the g-C3N4/AgBr shows similar absorption bands to g-C3N4. Analogously, the absorption peaks from CDs are not observed in the g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr composite due to the low content. As a result, the main peaks of g-C3N4 in the 1200–1650cm−1 and the breathing mode of tri-s-triazine units of g-C3N4 at 809cm−1 were both observed in g-C3N4/AgBr and g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr composites. The analogous phenomena have also been observed in previous reports [35].

The optical properties of the as-prepared samples are investigated by UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4b, the band gaps of pure AgBr and g-C3N4 are calculated to be 2.6 and 2.7eV, respectively, which are well consistent with previous reports [33]. When CDs are introduced into the composite, the light absorption of g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr in the visible light region enhances apparently as compared with AgBr/g-C3N4 owing to the strong visible-light absorption ability of CDs. In addition, the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and the pore size distribution curve of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CDs samples are shown in Fig. S1. The BET specific surface area of the g-C3N4/CDs sample is approximately (68.20m2/g), which is larger than that of g-C3N4 sample (57.09m2/g), indicating that the introduction of CDs into g-C3N4 is beneficial for boosting the specific surface area of the materials, and as a result it might provide more reactive sites for the adsorption and catalysis process. The pore-size distribution obtained using the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method reveals a narrow peak centered both at 2.6nm for g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CDs, which proves the micropores are presented in g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CDs, and the modification of carbon dots does not destroy the pore size of g-C3N4.

In order to examine the recombination rate of the photogenerated carriers of as-prepared samples, PL analysis was conducted. As shown in Fig. 5 , the PL emission intensities are in the order of g-C3N4 >g-C3N4/CDs>g-C3N4/AgBr>g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr. It is well known that the high PL emission intensity means the big recombination rate of the photogenerated charge carriers. Therefore, g-C3N4 with the strongest PL emission has the highest recombination rate. When CDs or AgBr are combined with g-C3N4, the PL emission intensities become much lower, indicating that the introduction of CDs or AgBr into g-C3N4 can inhibit the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. Compared to g-C3N4, g-C3N4/CDs and g-C3N4/AgBr, g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr shows the lowest emission intensity, suggesting that the highest separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs. Therefore, the insertion of CDs into photocatalysts can reduce the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, and thus enhance the photocatalytic performance.

The photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared samples were evaluated by the degradation of RhB as model pollutant under visible-light irradiation. Prior to irradiation, the reaction mixtures were stirred for 60min in the dark to reach an adsorption–desorption equilibrium between RhB and photocatalyst. As displayed in Fig. 6 a, after 40min visible-light irradiation, the RhB removal efficiencies over AgBr, g-C3N4, g-C3N4/CDs, g-C3N4/AgBr, g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-1, g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-2, g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-3, g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-4 and g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-5 are 55%, 44%, 68%, 75%, 87%, 91%, 96%, 90% and 82%, respectively. This result indicates that the ternary composites of g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr show higher photocatalytic activity than single AgBr, g-C3N4 as well as binary composites of g-C3N4/CDs and g-C3N4/AgBr, and the g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-3 sample exhibits the best photocatalytic degradation performance. As shown in Fig. 6b, the photocatalytic reaction rate can be described by the pseudo-first-order kinetic: −ln (C/C 0)= kt, where t (min) is the irradiation time and k (min−1) is the apparent reaction rate constant. It can be seen from Fig. 6c that g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-3 possesses the highest rate constant (0.07648min−1), which is about 4.0, 5.3, 2.7 and 2.3 times higher than that of AgBr (0.01934min−1), g-C3N4 (0.01434min−1), g-C3N4/CDs (0.02867min−1) and g-C3N4/AgBr (0.0339min−1). Obviously, the photocatalytic performance of the binary g-C3N4/AgBr can be further enhanced by the introduction of CDs into the system. In addition, it is worth noting that with the increasing content of CDs, the photocatalytic activity of the ternary g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr photocatalysts increases first and then decreases. Therefore, an appropriate amount of CDs in the ternary photocatalysts is also crucial for achieving the optimum photocatalytic efficiency. On one hand, too low content of CDs in the composites cannot effectively promote the transfer of photogenerated charge carriers and improve the visible-light absorption, leading to a relatively low catalytic efficiency. On the other hand, too much CDs inserted into the system possibly act as the recombination centers, which reduce the photocatalytic efficiency. The stability of a photocatalyst is also important to its practical application. As shown in Fig. 6d, the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-3 has no obvious decrease after four photodegradation cycles, demonstrating that the g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr photocatalyst has a high photocatalytic stability.

The photoelectrochemical analysis is a powerful technique to study the interfacial charge transfer and recombination rates for photocatalysts. Fig. 7 shows the transient photocurrent responses of g-C3N4, AgBr, g-C3N4/CDs, g-C3N4/AgBr and g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-3 under intermittent visible-light irradiation for five on–off cycles. Obviously, the g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-3 presents the highest photocurrent response (14.02μA/cm2), which is about 42 times of g-C3N4 (0.33μA/cm2), 29 times of g-C3N4/CDs (0.48μA/cm2), 2.3 times of AgBr (6.22μA/cm2) and 1.6 times of g-C3N4/AgBr (8.86μA/cm2). All above results indicate that the g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr composite possesses better separation efficiency for the photoinduced charge carriers than g-C3N4, g-C3N4/CDs, AgBr and g-C3N4/AgBr, which is consistent with the PL spectroscopy results, further demonstrating that carbon dots can efficiently boost the interfacial charge transfer.

As we all known, the
O2 −, h+ and
OH are three major reactive species which play a key role in photocatalytic reaction process. In order to investigate the dominant active species in the photocatalytic process, the trapping experiments are carried out, as depicted in Fig. 8 . Benzoquinone (BZQ), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (Na2-EDTA) and isopropanol (IPA) are employed to act as
O2 −, h+ and
OH scavenger, respectively [35]. Fig. 8 shows the photodegradation of RhB with g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr-3 photocatalyst after adding various trapping scavengers. It can be seen that the introduction of BZQ into the photocatalytic system largely reduces the photodegradation efficiencies of RhB, which means that
O2 − species act as the dominant reactive species in the photocatalytic reaction. When Na2-EDTA is introduced into the system, the degradation efficiency decreases to some extent, which means that h+ also has an effect on the photocatalytic efficiency. However, when IPA is introduced into the system, the photocatalytic efficiency shows only a slight decrease, indicating that the
OH species play a very small role in the photocatalytic process. For the g-C3N4 with a band gap of 2.7eV, the VB is estimated to be 1.4eV and the CB is about −1.3eV [11]. The electrons produced on g-C3N4 are about −1.3eV, which is more negative than −0.44eV to reduce O2 into
O2 −, as a result,
O2 − species can be generated to act as the active species. Similarly, for the AgBr with a band gap of 2.6eV, the VB is estimated to be 1.7eV and the CB is about −0.9eV [30]. The electrons produced on AgBr are also negative enough to reduce O2 to yield
O2 − reactive species. Thus,
O2 − is the main active species in the photocatalytic system. Meanwhile, the VBs of both AgBr and g-C3N4 are more positive than 0.95eV [36] to oxidize RhB molecules directly, so that h+ plays a part in the photocatalytic process. In addition, the VBs of both g-C3N4 and AgBr are not more positive than 2.4eV to oxidize OH− directly into
OH radicals, and only a very small amount of
OH active species were generated through oxidizing water molecule by
O2− radicals. As a result,
OH species play a very small role in the photocatalytic process.

The reactive species evolved in the process of photocatalytic reaction was further investigated with the ESR technique by DMPO. As shown in Fig. 9 a, when exposed to light irradiation, a four-line ESR signal with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1 could be detected for g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr photocatalyst, which is characteristic of DMPO-
O2− adduct. No signal of DMPO-
O2 − was found in the dark, proving
O2 − radicals were generated under light irradiation. From Fig. 9b, a four-line ESR signal of DMPO-
OH with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 could be detected under light irradiation, indicating the presence of
OH species. Considering the relatively weaker intensity of the DMPO-
OH signal, it can be concluded that
OH species plays less important role in the photocatalytic process as compared with
O2 − radicals. This result is consistent with the trapping experiments shown above.

The possible photocatalytic mechanism of the as-prepared ternary g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr photocatalyst with enhanced performance is proposed. As shown in Fig. 10 , since the CB of g-C3N4 is more negative than that of AgBr, the photogenerated electrons in the CB of g-C3N4 can transfer to the CB of AgBr. Simultaneously, since the VB of AgBr is more positive than that of g-C3N4, the photogenerated holes can transfer from the VB of AgBr to the VB of g-C3N4. When CDs are deposited onto the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets, the CDs can play three roles in the photocatalytic process as follows: Firstly, according to previous reports, the LUMO level of CDs is in the range of 4.2–4.4eV [37] and the conduction band (CB) potential of g-C3N4 is 3.14eV versus absolute vacuum scale. Therefore, in theory, electron transfer from g-C3N4 to CDs is thermodynamically favorable, which makes electrons on g-C3N4 easily transfer to CDs. Secondly, CDs can act as electron transfer mediator to promote the electron transfer from g-C3N4 to AgBr, which improves the separation of electron-hole pairs. In addition, because CDs possesses upconversion photoluminescence properties, they are capable of absorbing a longer wavelength visible light and then emit a shorter wavelength light, leading to exciting g-C3N4 to form more charge carriers, which can also contribute to the enhanced photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr.

In summary, a new g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr ternary photocatalyst has been successfully prepared by introducing CDs onto the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets, followed by the in-situ growth of AgBr nanoparticles. The g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr photocatalysts show excellent visible-light driven photocatalytic performance for RhB degradation. In the photocatalytic process of g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr, CDs act as the charge transmission bridge between g-C3N4 and AgBr, and effectively promote the separation of electrons and holes. In addition, the upconversion photoluminescence property of CDs makes the photocatalysts utilize visible light more efficiently. As a result, the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr is largely enhanced as compared to g-C3N4/AgBr. This study further demonstrates that the development of CDs inserted photocatalytsts could be a promising approach to high-performance visible-light driven photocatalysts for environmental and energy applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51272094 and 51602129), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20150507), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Nos. 2015M580392 and 2015M580393).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2017.04.087.

ce:e-component.ce:label: Supplementary Fig. S1 and Table S1
